questions.stem: A production function shows the relationship between which of the following?
options: The price of a good and the quantity demanded
The quantities of inputs used and the maximum output they can produce
questions.explanation: A production function describes how the maximum output of a good depends on the quantities of factors of production (inputs) used, given the state of technology.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: In the short run, at least one factor of production is which of the following?
questions.explanation: The short run is the period over which at least one input (often capital or land) is fixed and cannot be varied. In the long run all factors can be varied.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The marginal product of labour is best defined as which of the following?
options: Total output divided by the number of workers
The extra output produced by employing one more unit of labour
The wage paid to the last worker
The total output of all workers
questions.explanation: Marginal product of labour is the change in total output resulting from adding one more unit of labour, $MP_L = \Delta TP / \Delta L$, holding other inputs fixed.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The average product of labour is calculated as which of the following?
options: The change in total product from one more worker
The wage rate times the number of workers
Total fixed cost divided by output
Total product divided by the number of units of labour
questions.explanation: Average product of labour is total product divided by the quantity of labour, $AP_L = TP / L$. It measures output per worker.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Total product rises from 10 units with 1 worker to 22 units with 2 workers. The marginal product of the second worker is:
questions.explanation: Marginal product of the second worker is the change in total product, $22 - 10 = 12$ units.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If total product is 30 units when 3 workers are employed, the average product of labour is:
questions.explanation: Average product equals total product divided by labour, $30 / 3 = 10$ units per worker.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The law of diminishing returns (diminishing marginal product) states that as more of a variable factor is added to a fixed factor, eventually which of the following happens?
options: The marginal product of the variable factor begins to fall
questions.explanation: With at least one factor fixed, adding more of a variable factor (such as labour) eventually causes the marginal product of that factor to decline. This is the law of diminishing marginal returns, a short-run idea.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Total fixed cost (TFC) is best described as which of the following?
options: Cost that is zero when output is zero
Cost that varies directly with output
Cost that does not change as output changes in the short run
The cost of raw materials only
questions.explanation: Fixed costs, such as rent on premises or insurance, do not change with the level of output in the short run. They must be paid even if output is zero.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Total variable cost (TVC) is best described as which of the following?
options: The same as total fixed cost
Cost that changes as the level of output changes
Cost paid only when the firm shuts down
Cost that stays the same at all outputs
questions.explanation: Variable costs, such as raw materials and wages of production workers, rise as output rises and fall as output falls. They are zero when output is zero.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Total cost (TC) is equal to which of the following?
options: Total fixed cost plus total variable cost
questions.explanation: Total cost is the sum of total fixed cost and total variable cost, $TC = TFC + TVC$, at each level of output.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Average total cost (ATC) is calculated as which of the following?
options: Total variable cost divided by total fixed cost
Total cost divided by the quantity of output
questions.explanation: Average total cost is total cost per unit of output, $ATC = TC / Q$. It can also be found as the sum of average fixed cost and average variable cost.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If total cost is 160 when output is 8 units, average total cost is:
questions.explanation: Average total cost equals total cost divided by output, $160 / 8 = 20$ per unit.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Marginal cost (MC) is best defined as which of the following?
options: The average of fixed and variable cost
The addition to total cost from producing one more unit of output
The cost of the fixed factor
questions.explanation: Marginal cost is the extra cost of producing one additional unit, $MC = \Delta TC / \Delta Q$. Because fixed cost does not change, marginal cost also equals the change in variable cost.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Total cost rises from 160 to 180 when output increases from 8 to 9 units. The marginal cost of the ninth unit is:
questions.explanation: Marginal cost of the ninth unit is the change in total cost, $180 - 160 = 20$.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Average fixed cost (AFC) as output increases will do which of the following?
questions.explanation: Since total fixed cost is constant, spreading it over more units of output means average fixed cost falls continuously as output rises, getting ever closer to zero but never reaching it.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The marginal cost curve cuts the average total cost curve and the average variable cost curve at which point?
options: Where average fixed cost is zero
questions.explanation: Marginal cost intersects both average total cost and average variable cost at their minimum points. While MC is below an average curve it pulls the average down; once MC rises above it the average rises, so they meet at the bottom.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If total fixed cost is 100 and output is 10 units, average fixed cost is:
questions.explanation: Average fixed cost equals total fixed cost divided by output, $100 / 10 = 10$ per unit.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The short-run average total cost curve is typically U-shaped. The rising part of the curve is mainly due to which of the following?
options: The law of diminishing marginal returns raising marginal and average costs
An increase in the number of firms
questions.explanation: As output expands with a fixed factor, diminishing returns eventually raise marginal cost, which pulls average variable and average total cost up. This produces the rising right hand part of the U-shaped cost curve.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The difference between average total cost and average variable cost at any output equals which of the following?
questions.explanation: Since $ATC = AVC + AFC$, the vertical gap between the ATC and AVC curves is average fixed cost. This gap narrows as output rises because average fixed cost falls.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Returns to scale are a long-run concept that considers what happens to output when which of the following occurs?
options: Only the fixed factor is increased
All inputs are increased in the same proportion
questions.explanation: Returns to scale describe how output responds when all inputs are changed together by the same proportion. This is possible only in the long run, when every factor is variable.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If a firm doubles all its inputs and output more than doubles, the firm is experiencing which of the following?
questions.explanation: When a proportional increase in all inputs leads to a more than proportional increase in output, the firm has increasing returns to scale, often associated with economies of scale.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If a firm doubles all inputs and output exactly doubles, the firm is experiencing which of the following?
questions.explanation: When output changes in exactly the same proportion as all inputs, the firm has constant returns to scale. Doubling every input doubles output.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Economies of scale refer to which of the following?
options: A fall in long-run average cost as the scale of output increases
A rise in average cost as output falls
A rise in marginal product in the short run
questions.explanation: Economies of scale occur when long-run average cost falls as the firm produces on a larger scale, for example through bulk buying, specialisation, or spreading overheads. Diseconomies of scale are the opposite.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following is the clearest example of a fixed cost for a manufacturing firm in the short run?
options: The annual rent on the factory building
The electricity used to run machines
The wages of hourly production workers
questions.explanation: Annual rent on premises does not vary with how much the firm produces in the short run, so it is a fixed cost. Raw materials, hourly wages, and machine electricity all rise with output and are variable costs.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: When the marginal product of labour is rising, what is happening to the marginal cost of output?
questions.explanation: Marginal cost and marginal product move inversely. When each extra worker adds more output (rising marginal product), the extra cost per unit of output falls. So rising marginal product means falling marginal cost.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The total product curve, as more of a variable input is added, typically rises at an increasing rate, then at a decreasing rate, and may finally fall. The portion where it rises at a decreasing rate corresponds to which of the following?
options: Falling but still positive marginal product
questions.explanation: When total product rises at a decreasing rate, each extra unit of input adds less than the one before, so marginal product is positive but falling. This is the region of diminishing marginal returns before total product peaks.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics
